Title: Bubble Sort
Description: A simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order, bubbling the largest element to the end in each pass.
Explanation:
Bubble Sort is one of the simplest sorting algorithms. It works by repeatedly stepping through the array, comparing adjacent elements, and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. After each full pass, the largest unsorted element 'bubbles up' to its correct position at the end.

**How it works step by step:**
- Start from index 0, compare `arr[0]` and `arr[1]`. If `arr[0] > arr[1]`, swap them.
- Move to index 1, compare `arr[1]` and `arr[2]`. Swap if needed.
- Continue until the end of the array — the largest element is now at the last position.
- Repeat the process for the remaining unsorted portion (n-1 elements, then n-2, etc.).
- Stop when no swaps occur in a full pass — the array is sorted.

**Optimization:** Track a `swapped` flag. If no swaps happen in a full pass, the array is already sorted and you can exit early. This makes the best case O(n) for an already-sorted array.

**When to use it:** Bubble Sort is rarely used in production due to its O(n²) average complexity. However it is ideal for teaching sorting concepts because it is the most intuitive algorithm to visualize.
